'''2011 Syrian protests''' are expected to begin on February 4, 2011 as Ssocial media mobilises the people of [[Syria]] for rallies demanding freedom, [[human rights]] and the end to emergency law, scheduled for Friday and Saturday February4 and February 5 in front of the parliament in [[Damascus]] and at Syrian embassies internationally.<ref>http://english.aljazeera.net/news/middleeast/2011/02/201122171649677912.html</ref> The planned protests has been named 'Day of Rage'.<ref>http://www.aolnews.com/2011/01/31/syria-protests-set-for-feb-5-will-the-day-of-rage-mirror-eg/</ref> President [[Bashar al-Assad]] has declared that his nation is immune from the kinds of mass protests taking place in Egypt.http://www.foxnews.com/opinion/2011/02/02/middle-east-protests-syria-egypt/

[[Category:Syria]]

The '''2011 Syrian protests''' are expected to begin on February 4, [[2011]] as social media mobilises the people of [[Syria]] for rallies demanding freedom, [[human rights]] and the end to emergency law, scheduled for Friday and Saturday February 4 and February 5 in front of the parliament in [[Damascus]] and at Syrian embassies internationally.<ref>[http://english.aljazeera.net/news/middleeast/2011/02/201122171649677912.html "Calls for weekend protests in Syria"].</ref> The planned protests has been named "Day of Rage".<ref>[http://www.aolnews.com/2011/01/31/syria-protests-set-for-feb-5-will-the-day-of-rage-mirror-eg/ AOL News].</ref> President [[Bashar al-Assad]] has declared that his nation is immune from the kinds of mass protests taking place in Egypt.<ref>[http://www.foxnews.com/opinion/2011/02/02/middle-east-protests-syria-egypt/ FOX News opinion].</ref>

Following the [[2010-2011 Tunisian Revolution]], [[Syria]] witnessed minor incidents.

==Self Immolation of Hasan Ali Akleh==
On 26 January 2011, Hasan Ali Akleh from [[Al-Hasakah]] poured gasoline and burned himself in the same way as Mohamed Bouazizi. According to eyewitnesses, the action was "a protest against the [[Syrian government]]".<ref name="Hasan Ali Akleh 1">{{cite web|url=http://www.free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37797 |title=Information on the death of a young man who burned himself in Al Hasakah |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref><ref name="Hasan Ali Akleh 2">{{cite web|url=http://www.metransparent.com/spip.php?page=article&id_article=12661&lang=ar |title=Syrian suicider is "Hasan Ali Akleh". Damascus has banned a demonstration in support of Egypt |publisher=metransparent.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref> On 29 January 2011, news channel [[Al Arabiya]] reported that Internet connection was cut off, that was quickly denied by official sources.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.dp-news.com/pages/detail.aspx?l=2&articleId=72236 |title=Internet is Available across Syria |publisher=dp-news.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref> 

==Ar-Raqqah Protest==
On 28 January 2011, a demonstration was held in [[Ar-Raqqah]] during the evening, in protest against the killing of two soldiers of [[Kurdish people|Kurdish]] descent.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37803 |title=Demonstration in Ar-Raqqa, Syria |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref>

==President Assad Interview==
On 31 January 2011, the ''[[Wall Street Journal]]'' ran an exclusive interview with [[Bashar al-Assad]], [[President]] of [[Syria]], in which he said it's time to reform. In a comment on the protests in [[Protests in Egypt|Egypt]], [[2010–2011 Tunisian uprising|Tunisia]] and [[2011 Yemeni protests|Yemen]], he said a "new era" is coming to the [[Middle East]], and that Arab rulers would need to do more to accommodate their people's rising political and economic aspirations.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://online.wsj.com/article/SB10001424052748703833204576114712441122894.html |title=Interview With Syrian President Bashar al-Assad |publisher=wsj.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-31}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://online.wsj.com/article/SB10001424052748704832704576114340735033236.html |title=Syria Strongman: Time for 'Reform' |publisher=wsj.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-31}}</ref>

==February 4th, Day of Rage==
On 3 February another "Day of rage"  was called for in Syria from 4–5 February on [[social media]] sites Facebook and Twitter. Protesters demand reform in the government, however most protests so far have remained outside of Syria and have been small.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/41400687/ns/world_news-mideastn_africa/ |title='Day of rage' protest urged in Syria - World news - Mideast/N. Africa - msnbc.com |publisher=MSNBC |date= |accessdate=2011-02-03}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.aysor.am/en/news/2011/02/03/syrian-facebook-twitter/ |title=“Day of Rage” planned for Syria; protests scheduled for Feb 4-5 - aysor.am - Hot news from Armenia |publisher=aysor.am |date= |accessdate=2011-02-03}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://freedomist.com/2011/01/30/syran-anger-day-planned-feb-5-2011/|title=Syrian Anger Day Planned Feb 5, 2011|author=William R Collier jr|date=January 30, 2011|publisher=The Freedomist|accessdate=January 31, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://topsy.com/freedomist.com/2011/01/30/syran-anger-day-planned-feb-5-2011/ |title=Syrian Anger Day Planned Feb 5, 2011 | The Freedomist |publisher=TOPSY |date= |accessdate=2011-02-01}}</ref>

The protests were expected to begin on 4 February 2011 as [[social media]] mobilised the people of [[Syria]] for rallies demanding freedom, [[human rights]] and the end to the [[state of emergency]]. Protests were also scheduled for 5 February in front of the parliament in [[Damascus]] and at Syrian embassies internationally.<ref>[http://english.aljazeera.net/news/middleeast/2011/02/201122171649677912.html "Calls for weekend protests in Syria"].</ref> The planned protests had been named "Day of Rage."<ref>[http://www.aolnews.com/2011/01/31/syria-protests-set-for-feb-5-will-the-day-of-rage-mirror-eg/ AOL News].</ref> The only known action took place February 5, where hundreds of protesters in [[Al-Hasakah]] participated in a mass demonstration, calling for the [[Bashar al-Assad| President]]'s departure. Syrian authorities have arrested dozens and the demonstration was triggered quickly.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37849 |title=Demonstration on the day of anger in Hasaka and Syrian authorities arrested dozens |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-15}}</ref> President [[Bashar al-Assad]] has declared that his state is immune from the kinds of mass protests taking place in Egypt.<ref>[http://www.foxnews.com/opinion/2011/02/02/middle-east-protests-syria-egypt/ FOX News opinion].</ref> ''Al Jazeera'' also reported plans to increase security for the planned "days of rage." Days before the planned protests, Syrian authorities arrested several political activists, such as businessman Ghassan al-Najar, leader of the Islamic Democratic movement,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.elaph.com/Web/news/2011/2/629499.html |title=Arrest of leader of the Islamic Democratic movement in Syria |publisher=elaph.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37876 |title=Transfer of activist Ghassan Najar to Adra prison after being referred for trial |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> the writer Ali al-Abdallah,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37861 |title=Start of the trial exhibits and the Syrian writer Ali Abdullah |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> Abbas Abbas, from [[Syrian Communist Party]]<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37788 |title=Jailed prominent Syrian opposition for seven and a half years |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> and several other political personalities of Kurdish backgrounds, such as Adnan Mustafa.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37802 |title=Syrian authorities detain national identity Adnan Mustafa Abu Ammar |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> Suhair Atassi, who runs the banned [[Jamal al-Atassi| Jamal Atassi Forum]], called for political reforms and the reinstatement of civil rights, as well as an end to the emergency law in place since 1963.<ref>{{cite magazine|url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/2011/02/201129135657367367.html |title=Q&A: Syrian activist Suhair Atassi |publisher=Al Jazeera English |date=2011-02-09 |accessdate=2011-02-13}}</ref> However, no protests occurred on either date.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.upi.com/Top_News/Special/2011/02/04/Syria-quiet-on-day-of-rage/UPI-58781296853501/ |title=Syria quiet on day of rage |publisher=UPI.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-08}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.irishtimes.com/newspaper/world/2011/0205/1224289070429.html |title=Call for Syrian 'day of rage' as planned protests fail to ignite - The Irish Times - Sat, Feb 05, 2011 |publisher=The Irish Times |date= |accessdate=2011-02-08}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last=Karam |first=Zeina |url=http://www.miamiherald.com/2011/02/05/2052033/syria-escapes-day-of-rage-protests.html |title=Syria escapes 'Day of Rage' protests - World Wires |publisher=MiamiHerald.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-08}}</ref> Though internet services were said to have been curbed, ''Facebook'' and ''Youtube'' were reported to have been restored on 8 February.<ref>http://www.nytimes.com/2011/02/10/world/middleeast/10syria.html</ref> Suggestions were made that easing the ban could be a way to track activists.<ref>{{cite web|author=Jillian York |url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/opinion/2011/02/2011212122746819907.html |title=Unblocking Syria's social media |publisher=Al Jazeera English |date= |accessdate=2011-02-13}}</ref> After the failure of attempts to a "day of rage," ''[[Al Jazeera]]'' called the country "a kingdom of silence" and identified key factors for stability within Syria as being strict security measures, the popularity of President [[Bashar al-Assad]], and fears of potential sectarian violence in the aftermath of a government ouster (akin to neighbouring [[Iraqi insurgency|Iraq]]).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/features/2011/02/201129103121562395.html |title=Syria: 'A kingdom of silence' |publisher=aljazeera.net |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref>

==Tal al-Mallohi Sentenced==
On February 14, blogger and student [[Tal al-Mallohi]] was sentenced to five years in prison for spying for the United States. Washington denied these allegations and asked for the release Tal immediately. February 15, under pressure from human rights organizations, the Syrian government released ''Ghassan al-Najjar'', a "veteran Islamist activist" after he went on hunger strike following his arrest for calling for mass protests.<ref>{{cite news | url = http://www.reuters.com/article/2011/02/16/us-protests-idUSTRE71F41K20110216?pageNumber=2 | title = Egypt-inspired protests gain pace across region | first = Paul | last = Taylor | date = 2011-02-16 | publisher = Reuters }}</ref> February 16, regime critic and director of the Organisation for Democracy and Freedom in Syria (ODFS) [[Ribal al-Assad]], the son of [[Rifaat al-Assad]] and cousin to Syrian President [[Bashar al-Assad]] held a press conference in London, where he made it clear that he "does not want to see a Syrian revolution, but a peaceful change of power".<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.elaph.com/Web/news/2011/2/632409.html |title=Opposition leader Ribal Al-Assad is optimistic to changes in Syria |publisher=elaph.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-16}}</ref>

==February 17th Down Town Damascus protest==
On February 17, a fight took place in central [[Damascus]], between a shop owner and traffic police. Everything took a new turn when three police officers began attacking the man and beat him severely. Several people gathered around and blocked the road, while shouting "the Syrian people will not be pushed down". An eyewitness estimated the number to more than 1,500 demonstrators. Secret police officers arrived quickly to the scene along with several "big names" and finally [[Minister of the Interior]] who scared away the demonstrators. The [[Minister of the Interior| minister]] took the shop owner in his car and promised to investigate what had happened.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37926 |title=After the attack on a young man, beating and humiliation: the hundreds of Syrians demonstrating in Harika |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref> A couple of hours later, several video versions were posted on YouTube.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qDHLsU-ik_Y |title=Demonstration in Damscus Syria |publisher=Youtube |date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref>

==Anti-government graffiti==
On February 19, several Syrian websites reported widespread anti-government graffiti across Damascus and other major cities. <ref>http://www.sooryoon.net/exclusive/%D8%B4%D8%A8%D8%A7%D8%A8-%D8%B3%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%8A%D8%A9-%D9%8A%D9%83%D8%AA%D8%A8%D9%88%D9%86-%D8%B4%D8%B9%D8%A7%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AA-%D8%AA%D8%B7%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A8-%D8%A8%D8%B1%D8%AD%D9%8A%D9%84-%D8%A8/</ref>

[[Secret police]] officers arrived quickly to the scene along with several government officials and finally Minister of the Interior who  dispersed the demonstrators.<ref name="ROBERT MACKEY"> {{cite news  
| last=  MACKEY
| first= ROBERT  
| url=  http://thelede.blogs.nytimes.com/2011/02/17/latest-updates-on-middle-east-protests-4/?partner=rss&emc=rss
| title=  Feb. 17 Updates on Middle East Protests
| publisher=  [[nytimes.com]]
| date=  February 17, 2011
| accessdate= }} </ref> The minister took the shop owner in his car and promised to investigate what had happened.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37926 |title=After the attack on a young man, beating and humiliation: the hundreds of Syrians demonstrating in Harika |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref> A couple of hours later, several video versions were posted on [[YouTube]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qDHLsU-ik_Y |title=Demonstration in Damscus Syria |publisher=[[YouTube]]|date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref>

The '''2011 Syrian protests''' are a series of minor protests happening in [[Syria]] which began on 26 January 2011 and are [[Tunisia Effect|influenced]] by [[2010–2011 Middle East and North Africa protests|other protests in the region]]

==Background==
On 31 January 2011, the ''[[Wall Street Journal]]'' ran an exclusive interview with [[Bashar al-Assad]], [[President]] of [[Syria]], in which he said it's time to reform. In a comment on the protests in [[Protests in Egypt|Egypt]], [[2010–2011 Tunisian uprising|Tunisia]] and [[2011 Yemeni protests|Yemen]], he said a "new era" is coming to the [[Middle East]], and that Arab rulers would need to do more to accommodate their people's rising political and economic aspirations.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://online.wsj.com/article/SB10001424052748703833204576114712441122894.html |title=Interview With Syrian President Bashar al-Assad |publisher=wsj.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-31}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://online.wsj.com/article/SB10001424052748704832704576114340735033236.html |title=Syria Strongman: Time for 'Reform' |publisher=wsj.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-31}}</ref> However, president [[Bashar al-Assad|Al-Assad]] has also declared that his state is immune from the kinds of mass protests that have taken place in Egypt.<ref>[http://www.foxnews.com/opinion/2011/02/02/middle-east-protests-syria-egypt/ FOX News opinion].</ref>

== Protests ==
=== Timeline ===
==== 26-27 January ====
On 26 January 2011, Hasan Ali Akleh from [[Al-Hasakah]] poured gasoline and burned himself in the same way as Mohamed Bouazizi. According to eyewitnesses, the action was "a protest against the [[Syrian government]]".<ref name="Hasan Ali Akleh 1">{{cite web|url=http://www.free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37797 |title=Information on the death of a young man who burned himself in Al Hasakah |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref><ref name="Hasan Ali Akleh 2">{{cite web|url=http://www.metransparent.com/spip.php?page=article&id_article=12661&lang=ar |title=Syrian suicider is "Hasan Ali Akleh". Damascus has banned a demonstration in support of Egypt |publisher=metransparent.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref> 

==== 28 January ====
On 28 January 2011, a demonstration was held in [[Ar-Raqqah]] during the evening, in protest against the killing of two soldiers of [[Kurdish people|Kurdish]] descent.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37803 |title=Demonstration in Ar-Raqqa, Syria |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref>

==== 3-16 February ====
On 3 February another "Day of rage"  was called for in Syria from 4–5 February on [[social media]] sites Facebook and Twitter. Protesters demand reform in the government, however most protests so far have remained outside of Syria and have been small.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/41400687/ns/world_news-mideastn_africa/ |title='Day of rage' protest urged in Syria - World news - Mideast/N. Africa - msnbc.com |publisher=MSNBC |date= |accessdate=2011-02-03}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.aysor.am/en/news/2011/02/03/syrian-facebook-twitter/ |title=“Day of Rage” planned for Syria; protests scheduled for Feb 4-5 - aysor.am - Hot news from Armenia |publisher=aysor.am |date= |accessdate=2011-02-03}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://freedomist.com/2011/01/30/syran-anger-day-planned-feb-5-2011/|title=Syrian Anger Day Planned Feb 5, 2011|author=William R Collier jr|date=January 30, 2011|publisher=The Freedomist|accessdate=January 31, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://topsy.com/freedomist.com/2011/01/30/syran-anger-day-planned-feb-5-2011/ |title=Syrian Anger Day Planned Feb 5, 2011 | The Freedomist |publisher=TOPSY |date= |accessdate=2011-02-01}}</ref> The protests were expected to begin on 4 February 2011 as [[social media]] mobilised the people of [[Syria]] for rallies demanding freedom, [[human rights]] and the end to the [[state of emergency]]. Protests were also scheduled for 5 February in front of the parliament in [[Damascus]] and at Syrian embassies internationally.<ref>[http://english.aljazeera.net/news/middleeast/2011/02/201122171649677912.html "Calls for weekend protests in Syria"].</ref> The only known action took place February 5, where hundreds of protesters in [[Al-Hasakah]] participated in a mass demonstration, calling for the [[Bashar al-Assad| President]]'s departure. Syrian authorities have arrested dozens and the demonstration was triggered quickly.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37849 |title=Demonstration on the day of anger in Hasaka and Syrian authorities arrested dozens |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-15}}</ref> Suhair Atassi, who runs the banned [[Jamal al-Atassi| Jamal Atassi Forum]], called for political reforms and the reinstatement of civil rights, as well as an end to the emergency law in place since 1963.<ref>{{cite magazine|url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/2011/02/201129135657367367.html |title=Q&A: Syrian activist Suhair Atassi |publisher=Al Jazeera English |date=2011-02-09 |accessdate=2011-02-13}}</ref> However, no protests occurred on either date.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.upi.com/Top_News/Special/2011/02/04/Syria-quiet-on-day-of-rage/UPI-58781296853501/ |title=Syria quiet on day of rage |publisher=UPI.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-08}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.irishtimes.com/newspaper/world/2011/0205/1224289070429.html |title=Call for Syrian 'day of rage' as planned protests fail to ignite - The Irish Times - Sat, Feb 05, 2011 |publisher=The Irish Times |date= |accessdate=2011-02-08}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last=Karam |first=Zeina |url=http://www.miamiherald.com/2011/02/05/2052033/syria-escapes-day-of-rage-protests.html |title=Syria escapes 'Day of Rage' protests - World Wires |publisher=MiamiHerald.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-08}}</ref> Though internet services were said to have been curbed, ''Facebook'' and ''Youtube'' were reported to have been restored on 8 February.<ref name="JENNIFER PRESTON"> {{cite news  
| last=  PRESTON
| first=  JENNIFER 
| url=  http://www.nytimes.com/2011/02/10/world/middleeast/10syria.html?_r=1
| title=  Syria Restores Access to Facebook and YouTube
| publisher=  [[The New York Times]]
| date=  9 February 2011
| accessdate= }} </ref> Suggestions were made that easing the ban could be a way to track activists.<ref>{{cite web|author=Jillian York |url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/opinion/2011/02/2011212122746819907.html |title=Unblocking Syria's social media |publisher=Al Jazeera English |date= |accessdate=2011-02-13}}</ref> After the failure of attempts to a "day of rage," ''[[Al Jazeera]]'' called the country "a kingdom of silence" and identified key factors for stability within Syria as being strict security measures, the popularity of President [[Bashar al-Assad]], and fears of potential sectarian violence in the aftermath of a government ouster (akin to neighbouring [[Iraqi insurgency|Iraq]]).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/features/2011/02/201129103121562395.html |title=Syria: 'A kingdom of silence' |publisher=aljazeera.net |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref>

==== 17 February ====
{{External media
| video1 = {{YouTube|8Cwm5Ck2sSs|Protests in Damscus, Syria, February 17, 2011}}
}}
On February 17, a fight took place in central [[Damascus]], between a shop owner and traffic police. Everything took a new turn when three police officers began attacking the man and beat him severely. Several people gathered around and blocked the road, while shouting "the [[Syrian people]] will not be pushed down". An eyewitness estimated the number to more than 1,500 demonstrators. [[Secret police]] officers arrived quickly to the scene along with several government officials and finally Minister of the Interior who  dispersed the demonstrators.<ref name="ROBERT MACKEY"> {{cite news  
| last=  MACKEY
| first= ROBERT  
| url=  http://thelede.blogs.nytimes.com/2011/02/17/latest-updates-on-middle-east-protests-4/?partner=rss&emc=rss
| title=  Feb. 17 Updates on Middle East Protests
| publisher=  [[nytimes.com]]
| date=  February 17, 2011
| accessdate= }} </ref> The minister took the shop owner in his car and promised to investigate what had happened.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37926 |title=After the attack on a young man, beating and humiliation: the hundreds of Syrians demonstrating in Harika |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref> A couple of hours later, several video versions were posted on [[YouTube]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qDHLsU-ik_Y |title=Demonstration in Damscus Syria |publisher=[[YouTube]]|date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref>

==== 19 February ====
On February 19, several Syrian websites reported widespread anti-government [[graffiti]] across Damascus and other major cities.<ref name="sooryoon exclusive"> {{cite web
| url=  http://www.sooryoon.net/exclusive/%D8%B4%D8%A8%D8%A7%D8%A8-%D8%B3%D9%88%D8%B1%D9%8A%D8%A9-%D9%8A%D9%83%D8%AA%D8%A8%D9%88%D9%86-%D8%B4%D8%B9%D8%A7%D8%B1%D8%A7%D8%AA-%D8%AA%D8%B7%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%A8-%D8%A8%D8%B1%D8%AD%D9%8A%D9%84-%D8%A8/
| title= شباب سورية يكتبون شعارات تطالب برحيل بشار أسد ونظامه على جدران المدن الرئيسية  
| publisher=  sooryoon.net
| date=   2011/02/19
| accessdate =
}} </ref>

==== 22 February ====
On 22 February, 14 people were arrested and several more beaten by uniformed and plainclothes police following a peaceful sit-in outside the Libyan embassy in Damascus.<ref name=guardian240211> {{cite news| url=http://www.guardian.co.uk/world/2011/feb/24/syria-crackdown-protest-arrests-beatings| title=Syria clamps down on dissent with beatings and arrests| publisher=The Guardian| date=24 February 2011| accessdate=24 February 2011 }} </ref> On 23 February a demonstration in Damascus was violently dispersed, amid increasing reports of intimidation in the wake of unrest in neighbouring Arab countries.<ref name=guardian240211/>

On February 22, dozens of people gathered outside the Libyan embassy in Damascus to protest against the Libyan regime and wanted the ambassador to resign. Despite the peaceful message, there were several security officers on site. Everything took a new turn when the protesters started shouting "Corrupt, he who strikes his people", which created nervousness among the security forces who immediately asked people to leave to their homes. <ref> {{cite web
| url=  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UNLUCg-TrZU&feature=player_embedded#at=20
| title= الاعتصام امام السفارة الليبية دمشق-22-شباط  Part 5
| publisher=  Youtube
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref><ref> {{cite web
| url=  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GLkLqI07erk
| title= الاعتصام امام السفارة الليبية دمشق-22-شباط  Part 8
| publisher=  Youtube
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref>

==== 23 February ====
On February 23, attempted a professor at the highly screened [[University of Damascus]] start a revolt from within the University facilities, but he was met quickly by security police officers who beat him bloody while screaming at him "Do you want to teach? First, learn that al-Assad family are your masters, you child of a whore". The professor was thereafter taken by intelligence men. A short video from the event spread to different websites.<ref> {{cite web
| url=  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6GnOa371_Eg
| title=Syrian intelligence men
| publisher=  Youtube
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref>A similar video showing the torture of Syrian officers is widely used in social media where participants require a revolution in Syria.<ref> {{cite web
| url=  https://www.facebook.com/video/video.php?v=190837474274296
| title=Why We SHOULD Protest Against Bashar assad? [HQ]
| publisher=  Facebook
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref>

==== 24 February ====
On February 24, a new date for a Syrian "day of rage" was announced on March 15 under the name "the Syrian uprising".<ref> {{cite web
| url=  http://www.facebook.com/Syrian.Revolution
| title=The Syrian Revolution 2011 الثورة السورية ضد بشار الاسد
| publisher=  Facebook
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref><ref> {{cite web
| url=  http://www.facebook.com/DaysOfRage
| title=يوم الغضب السوري
| publisher=  Facebook
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref>

==Reaction==
On 29 January 2011, news channel [[Al Arabiya]] reported that Internet connection was cut off, that was quickly denied by official sources.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.dp-news.com/pages/detail.aspx?l=2&articleId=72236 |title=Internet is Available across Syria |publisher=dp-news.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref> 
''Al Jazeera'' reported increased security for the planned "Days of rage"{{Citation needed|date=February 2011}}. Days before the planned protests, Syrian authorities arrested several political activists, such as businessman Ghassan al-Najar, leader of the Islamic Democratic movement,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.elaph.com/Web/news/2011/2/629499.html |title=Arrest of leader of the Islamic Democratic movement in Syria |publisher=elaph.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37876 |title=Transfer of activist Ghassan Najar to Adra prison after being referred for trial |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> the writer Ali al-Abdallah,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37861 |title=Start of the trial exhibits and the Syrian writer Ali Abdullah |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> Abbas Abbas, from [[Syrian Communist Party]]<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37788 |title=Jailed prominent Syrian opposition for seven and a half years |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> and several other political personalities of Kurdish backgrounds, such as Adnan Mustafa.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37802 |title=Syrian authorities detain national identity Adnan Mustafa Abu Ammar |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref>

On February 14, blogger and student [[Tal al-Mallohi]] was sentenced to five years in prison for spying for the United States. Washington denied these allegations and asked for the release Tal immediately. February 15, under pressure from human rights organizations, the Syrian government released ''Ghassan al-Najjar'', a "veteran Islamist activist" after he went on hunger strike following his arrest for calling for mass protests.<ref>{{cite news | url = http://www.reuters.com/article/2011/02/16/us-protests-idUSTRE71F41K20110216?pageNumber=2 | title = Egypt-inspired protests gain pace across region | first = Paul | last = Taylor | date = 2011-02-16 | publisher = Reuters }}</ref> February 16, regime critic and director of the Organisation for Democracy and Freedom in Syria (ODFS) [[Ribal al-Assad]], the son of [[Rifaat al-Assad]] and cousin to Syrian President [[Bashar al-Assad]] held a press conference in London, where he made it clear that he "does not want to see a Syrian revolution, but a peaceful change of power".<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.elaph.com/Web/news/2011/2/632409.html |title=Opposition leader Ribal Al-Assad is optimistic to changes in Syria |publisher=elaph.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-16}}</ref>

| publisher=  [[The New York Times]]
| date=  9 February 2011
| accessdate= }} </ref> Suggestions were made that easing the ban could be a way to track activists.<ref>{{cite web|author=Jillian York |url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/opinion/2011/02/2011212122746819907.html |title=Unblocking Syria's social media |publisher=Al Jazeera English |date= |accessdate=2011-02-13}}</ref> After the failure of attempts to a "day of rage," ''[[Al Jazeera]]'' called the country "a kingdom of silence" and identified key factors for stability within Syria as being strict security measures, the popularity of President [[Bashar al-Assad]], and fears of potential sectarian violence in the aftermath of a government ouster (akin to neighbouring [[Iraqi insurgency|Iraq]]).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/features/2011/02/201129103121562395.html |title=Syria: 'A kingdom of silence' |publisher=aljazeera.net |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref>

==== 17 February ====
{{External media
| video1 = {{YouTube|8Cwm5Ck2sSs|Protests in Damscus, Syria, February 17, 2011}}
}}
On February 17, a fight took place in central [[Damascus]], between a shop owner and traffic police. Everything took a new turn when three police officers began attacking the man and beat him severely. Several people gathered around and blocked the road, while shouting "the [[Syrian people]] will not be pushed down". An eyewitness estimated the number to more than 1,500 demonstrators. [[Secret police]] officers arrived quickly to the scene along with several government officials and finally Minister of the Interior who  dispersed the demonstrators.<ref name="ROBERT MACKEY"> {{cite news  
| last=  MACKEY
| first= ROBERT  
| url=  http://thelede.blogs.nytimes.com/2011/02/17/latest-updates-on-middle-east-protests-4/?partner=rss&emc=rss
| title=  Feb. 17 Updates on Middle East Protests
| publisher=  [[nytimes.com]]
| date=  February 17, 2011
| accessdate= }} </ref> The minister took the shop owner in his car and promised to investigate what had happened.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37926 |title=After the attack on a young man, beating and humiliation: the hundreds of Syrians demonstrating in Harika |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref> A couple of hours later, several video versions were posted on [[YouTube]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qDHLsU-ik_Y |title=Demonstration in Damscus Syria |publisher=[[YouTube]]|date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref>

==== 22 February ====
On 22 February, 14 people were arrested and several more beaten by uniformed and plainclothes police following a peaceful sit-in outside the Libyan embassy in Damascus.<ref name=guardian240211> {{cite news| url=http://www.guardian.co.uk/world/2011/feb/24/syria-crackdown-protest-arrests-beatings| title=Syria clamps down on dissent with beatings and arrests| publisher=The Guardian| date=24 February 2011| accessdate=24 February 2011 }} </ref> On 23 February a demonstration in Damascus was violently dispersed, amid increasing reports of intimidation in the wake of unrest in neighbouring Arab countries.<ref name=guardian240211/>

On February 22, dozens of people gathered outside the Libyan embassy in Damascus to protest against the Libyan regime and wanted the ambassador to resign. Despite the peaceful message, there were several security officers on site. Everything took a new turn when the protesters started shouting "Corrupt, he who strikes his people", which created nervousness among the security forces who immediately asked people to leave to their homes. <ref> {{cite web
| url=  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UNLUCg-TrZU&feature=player_embedded#at=20
| title= الاعتصام امام السفارة الليبية دمشق-22-شباط  Part 5
| publisher=  Youtube
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref><ref> {{cite web
| url=  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GLkLqI07erk
| title= الاعتصام امام السفارة الليبية دمشق-22-شباط  Part 8
| publisher=  Youtube
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref>

==Reaction==
On 29 January 2011, news channel [[Al Arabiya]] reported that Internet connection was cut off, that was quickly denied by official sources.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.dp-news.com/pages/detail.aspx?l=2&articleId=72236 |title=Internet is Available across Syria |publisher=dp-news.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref> 
''Al Jazeera'' reported increased security for the planned "Days of rage"{{Citation needed|date=February 2011}}. Days before the planned protests, Syrian authorities arrested several political activists, such as businessman Ghassan al-Najar, leader of the Islamic Democratic movement,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.elaph.com/Web/news/2011/2/629499.html |title=Arrest of leader of the Islamic Democratic movement in Syria |publisher=elaph.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37876 |title=Transfer of activist Ghassan Najar to Adra prison after being referred for trial |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> the writer Ali al-Abdallah,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37861 |title=Start of the trial exhibits and the Syrian writer Ali Abdullah |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> Abbas Abbas, from [[Syrian Communist Party]]<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37788 |title=Jailed prominent Syrian opposition for seven and a half years |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref> and several other political personalities of Kurdish backgrounds, such as Adnan Mustafa.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37802 |title=Syrian authorities detain national identity Adnan Mustafa Abu Ammar |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref>

On February 14, blogger and student [[Tal al-Mallohi]] was sentenced to five years in prison for spying for the United States. Washington denied these allegations and asked for the release Tal immediately. February 15, under pressure from human rights organizations, the Syrian government released ''Ghassan al-Najjar'', a "veteran Islamist activist" after he went on hunger strike following his arrest for calling for mass protests.<ref>{{cite news | url = http://www.reuters.com/article/2011/02/16/us-protests-idUSTRE71F41K20110216?pageNumber=2 | title = Egypt-inspired protests gain pace across region | first = Paul | last = Taylor | date = 2011-02-16 | publisher = Reuters }}</ref> February 16, regime critic and director of the Organisation for Democracy and Freedom in Syria (ODFS) [[Ribal al-Assad]], the son of [[Rifaat al-Assad]] and cousin to Syrian President [[Bashar al-Assad]] held a press conference in London, where he made it clear that he "does not want to see a Syrian revolution, but a peaceful change of power".<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.elaph.com/Web/news/2011/2/632409.html |title=Opposition leader Ribal Al-Assad is optimistic to changes in Syria |publisher=elaph.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-16}}</ref>

The '''2011 Syrian protests''' are a series of minor protests happening in [[Syria]] which began on 26 January 2011 and are [[Tunisia Effect|influenced]] by [[2010–2011 Middle East and North Africa protests|other protests in the region]].

==Background==
On 31 January 2011, the ''[[Wall Street Journal]]'' ran an exclusive interview with [[Bashar al-Assad]], [[President]] of [[Syria]], in which he said it's time to reform. In a comment on the protests in [[Protests in Egypt|Egypt]], [[2010–2011 Tunisian uprising|Tunisia]] and [[2011 Yemeni protests|Yemen]], he said a "new era" is coming to the [[Middle East]], and that Arab rulers would need to do more to accommodate their people's rising political and economic aspirations.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://online.wsj.com/article/SB10001424052748703833204576114712441122894.html |title=Interview With Syrian President Bashar al-Assad |publisher=wsj.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-31}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://online.wsj.com/article/SB10001424052748704832704576114340735033236.html |title=Syria Strongman: Time for 'Reform' |publisher=wsj.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-31}}</ref> However, president [[Bashar al-Assad|Al-Assad]] has also declared that his state is immune from the kinds of mass protests that have taken place in Egypt.<ref>[http://www.foxnews.com/opinion/2011/02/02/middle-east-protests-syria-egypt/ FOX News opinion].</ref>

== Protests ==
=== Timeline ===
==== 26–28 January ====
On 26 January 2011, Hasan Ali Akleh from [[Al-Hasakah]] poured gasoline and burned himself in the same way as Mohamed Bouazizi. According to eyewitnesses, the action was "a protest against the [[Syrian government]]".<ref name="Hasan Ali Akleh 1">{{cite web|url=http://www.free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37797 |title=Information on the death of a young man who burned himself in Al Hasakah |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref><ref name="Hasan Ali Akleh 2">{{cite web|url=http://www.metransparent.com/spip.php?page=article&id_article=12661&lang=ar |title=Syrian suicider is "Hasan Ali Akleh". Damascus has banned a demonstration in support of Egypt |publisher=metransparent.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref> 

On 28 January 2011, a demonstration was held in [[Ar-Raqqah]] during the evening, in protest against the killing of two soldiers of [[Kurdish people|Kurdish]] descent.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37803 |title=Demonstration in Ar-Raqqa, Syria |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-01-30}}</ref>

==== 2 February ====
On 2 February, [[Human Rights Watch]] reported a group of 20 people in civilian clothing had beaten and dispersed 15 people who had been holding a candlelight vigil at [[Bab Tuma]] in Damascus for [[2011 Egyptian revolution| Egyptian demonstrators]].<ref name=guardian240211> {{cite news| url=http://www.guardian.co.uk/world/2011/feb/24/syria-crackdown-protest-arrests-beatings| title=Syria clamps down on dissent with beatings and arrests| publisher=The Guardian| date=24 February 2011| accessdate=24 February 2011 }} </ref>

==== 3–16 February ====

On 3 February another "Day of rage"  was called for in Syria from 4–5 February on [[social media]] sites Facebook and Twitter. Protesters demand reform in the government, however most protests so far have remained outside of Syria and have been small.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/41400687/ns/world_news-mideastn_africa/ |title='Day of rage' protest urged in Syria - World news - Mideast/N. Africa - msnbc.com |publisher=MSNBC |date= |accessdate=2011-02-03}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.aysor.am/en/news/2011/02/03/syrian-facebook-twitter/ |title=“Day of Rage” planned for Syria; protests scheduled for Feb 4-5 - aysor.am - Hot news from Armenia |publisher=aysor.am |date= |accessdate=2011-02-03}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://freedomist.com/2011/01/30/syran-anger-day-planned-feb-5-2011/|title=Syrian Anger Day Planned Feb 5, 2011|author=William R Collier jr|date=January 30, 2011|publisher=The Freedomist|accessdate=January 31, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://topsy.com/freedomist.com/2011/01/30/syran-anger-day-planned-feb-5-2011/ |title=Syrian Anger Day Planned Feb 5, 2011 | The Freedomist |publisher=TOPSY |date= |accessdate=2011-02-01}}</ref> The protests were expected to begin on 4 February 2011 as [[social media]] mobilised the people of [[Syria]] for rallies demanding freedom, [[human rights]] and the end to the [[state of emergency]]. Protests were also scheduled for 5 February in front of the parliament in [[Damascus]] and at Syrian embassies internationally.<ref>[http://english.aljazeera.net/news/middleeast/2011/02/201122171649677912.html "Calls for weekend protests in Syria"].</ref> 

The only known action took place February 5, where hundreds of protesters in [[Al-Hasakah]] participated in a mass demonstration, calling for the [[Bashar al-Assad| President]]'s departure. Syrian authorities have arrested dozens and the demonstration was triggered quickly.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37849 |title=Demonstration on the day of anger in Hasaka and Syrian authorities arrested dozens |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-15}}</ref> Suhair Atassi, who runs the banned [[Jamal al-Atassi| Jamal Atassi Forum]], called for political reforms and the reinstatement of civil rights, as well as an end to the emergency law in place since 1963.<ref>{{cite magazine|url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/2011/02/201129135657367367.html |title=Q&A: Syrian activist Suhair Atassi |publisher=Al Jazeera English |date=2011-02-09 |accessdate=2011-02-13}}</ref> However, no protests occurred on either date.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.upi.com/Top_News/Special/2011/02/04/Syria-quiet-on-day-of-rage/UPI-58781296853501/ |title=Syria quiet on day of rage |publisher=UPI.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-08}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.irishtimes.com/newspaper/world/2011/0205/1224289070429.html |title=Call for Syrian 'day of rage' as planned protests fail to ignite - The Irish Times - Sat, Feb 05, 2011 |publisher=The Irish Times |date= |accessdate=2011-02-08}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last=Karam |first=Zeina |url=http://www.miamiherald.com/2011/02/05/2052033/syria-escapes-day-of-rage-protests.html |title=Syria escapes 'Day of Rage' protests - World Wires |publisher=MiamiHerald.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-08}}</ref> Though internet services were said to have been curbed, ''Facebook'' and ''Youtube'' were reported to have been restored on 8 February.<ref name="JENNIFER PRESTON"> {{cite news  
| last=  PRESTON
| first=  JENNIFER 
| url=  http://www.nytimes.com/2011/02/10/world/middleeast/10syria.html?_r=1
| title=  Syria Restores Access to Facebook and YouTube
| publisher=  [[The New York Times]]
| date=  9 February 2011
| accessdate= }} </ref> Suggestions were made that easing the ban could be a way to track activists.<ref>{{cite web|author=Jillian York |url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/opinion/2011/02/2011212122746819907.html |title=Unblocking Syria's social media |publisher=Al Jazeera English |date= |accessdate=2011-02-13}}</ref> After the failure of attempts to a "day of rage," ''[[Al Jazeera]]'' called the country "a kingdom of silence" and identified key factors for stability within Syria as being strict security measures, the popularity of President [[Bashar al-Assad]], and fears of potential sectarian violence in the aftermath of a government ouster (akin to neighbouring [[Iraqi insurgency|Iraq]]).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/features/2011/02/201129103121562395.html |title=Syria: 'A kingdom of silence' |publisher=aljazeera.net |date= |accessdate=2011-02-12}}</ref>

==== 17 February ====
{{External media
| video1 = {{YouTube|8Cwm5Ck2sSs|Protests in Damscus, Syria, February 17, 2011}}
}}
On February 17, a spontaneous demonstration took place outside [[Al-Hamidiyah Souq]] in Damascus, in protest at the police beating of a local shop owner. Several people gathered around and blocked the road, while they chanted "The [[Syrian people]] will not be humiliated". An eyewitness estimated the number to more than 1,500 demonstrators. [[Secret police]] officers arrived quickly to the scene along with several government officials and finally Syria's interior minister who dispersed the demonstrators and promised an investigation.<ref name=guardian240211/><ref name="ROBERT MACKEY"> {{cite news  
| last=  MACKEY
| first= ROBERT  
| url=  http://thelede.blogs.nytimes.com/2011/02/17/latest-updates-on-middle-east-protests-4/?partner=rss&emc=rss
| title=  Feb. 17 Updates on Middle East Protest
| publisher=  [[nytimes.com]]
| date=  February 17, 2011
| accessdate= }} </ref> The minister took the shop owner in his car and promised to investigate what had happened.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://free-syria.com/loadarticle.php?articleid=37926 |title=After the attack on a young man, beating and humiliation: the hundreds of Syrians demonstrating in Harika |publisher=free-syria.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref> A couple of hours later, several video versions were posted on [[YouTube]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qDHLsU-ik_Y |title=Demonstration in Damscus Syria |publisher=[[YouTube]]|date= |accessdate=2011-02-17}}</ref>

==== 22–23 February ====
On February 22 , around 200 people gathered outside the Libyan embassy in [[Damascus]] to protest against the Libyan regime and wanted the ambassador to resign. Despite the peaceful message, there were nearly twice as many secret and uniformed police as protesters. The demonstrators carried placards reading "Freedom for the people" and "Down with [[Gaddafi]]". Everything took a new turn when the protesters started chanting "Traitors are those that beat their people.", which created nervousness among the security forces who immediately asked people to leave to their homes. 14 people were arrested and several more beaten by policemen. Some protesters were punched, kicked and beaten with sticks. All present had their identities recorded. Fourteen people were detained but later released.<ref name=guardian240211/>
<ref> {{cite web
| url=  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UNLUCg-TrZU&feature=player_embedded#at=20
| title= الاعتصام امام السفارة الليبية دمشق-22-شباط  Part 5
| publisher=  Youtube
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref><ref> {{cite web
| url=  http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GLkLqI07erk
| title= الاعتصام امام السفارة الليبية دمشق-22-شباط  Part 8
| publisher=  Youtube
| date=   2011/02/24
| accessdate =
}} </ref>

On February 23, during a routine session of the [[Parliament of Syria| Syrian parliament]], a member proposed that harsh [[State of emergency| emergency laws]] to be reviewed. The issue was not scheduled for discussion, and it came as a surprise. A pro-government official at the session recalled it with anger. The proposal was rapidly quashed, when the speaker put the proposed review to a vote in the chamber, none of the other 249 MPs supported it.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.thenational.ae/news/worldwide/middle-east/syrian-mp-calls-for-review-of-harsh-emergency-laws |title=Syrian MP calls for review of harsh emergency laws |publisher=The National |date= |accessdate=2011-03-10}}</ref>

==== 6–9 March ====
On March 6, a number of young boys, under 15, were arrested in [[Daraa]] because they have written infamous slogan "the people want to overthrow the regime" on the walls of the city.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.aljazeera.net/NR/exeres/394E1B63-488A-4FF1-BD39-0CD306261FFE.htm?GoogleStatID=9 |title=سوريا تعتقل شبانا في درعا |publisher=Al Jazeera |date= |accessdate=2011-03-06}}</ref> Libyan [[Anti-Gaddafi forces]] shot down two Syrian war planes in [[Ras Lanuf]], that was later denied by Syrian officials.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.economicvoice.com/libyan-rebels-shoot-down-two-syrian-war-planes/50017136#axzz1FwTDTTfU |title=Libyan rebels shoot down two Syrian war planes |publisher=Economic Voice |date= |accessdate=2011-03-07}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://syria-news.com/readnews.php?sy_seq=129725 |title=مصدر رسمي سوري ينفي مشاركة طيارين سوريين بالقتال في ليبيا |publisher=Syria-news.com |date= |accessdate=2011-03-07}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.commentarymagazine.com/2011/03/06/syrian-pilots-shot-down-in-libya/
 |title=Syrian pilots shot down in Libya? |publisher=Commentary Magazine |date= |accessdate=2011-03-06}}</ref> Four brothers that are Syrian opposition activists went missing shortly after passing out flyers in Beirut in front of the Syrian embassy, calling for a demonstration to oppose Syria's government.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.monstersandcritics.com/news/middleeast/news/article_1624021.php/Search-on-for-Syrian-opposition-activists-missing-in-Lebanon |title=Search on for Syrian opposition activists missing in Lebanon |publisher=Middle East News |date= |accessdate=2011-03-06}}</ref>[[Time (magazine)| Time]] says that the commitment can still be found among the Syrian youth, but what is needed is a starting point.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,2057454,00.html |title=The Youth of Syria: The Rebels Are on Pause |publisher=Time.com |date= |accessdate=2011-03-06}}</ref> [[Ribal al-Assad]] says that it is almost time for Syria to be next [[Domino theory| domino]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://english.aljazeera.net/indepth/opinion/2011/03/20113482455647372.html |title=Is Syria the next domino? |publisher=Al Jazeera |date= |accessdate=2011-03-06}}</ref>

On March 7, 13 Syrian political prisoners have gone on [[hunger strike]] to protest against "political detentions and oppression" in Syria. They demanded an end to political arrests, the removal of injustices and the restitution of rights that have been removed from civil and political life<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.jpost.com/Headlines/Article.aspx?id=211153 |title=Syrian prisoners launch hunger strike |publisher=Jerusalem Post |date= |accessdate=2011-03-07}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.marxist.com/syria-prisoners-of-conscience-declare-hunger-strike.htm |title=Syria: Prisoners of Conscience in Damascus Central Prison declare hunger strike |publisher=marxist.com |date= |accessdate=2011-03-09}}</ref>

On March 8, Syrian authorities have released Haitham al-Maleh, an 80-year-old former judge, one of [[Bashar al-Assad]]'s most outspoken critics, under an amnesty marking the anniversary of the [[1963 Syrian coup d'état| 1963 coup]] which brought the [[Baath Party]] to power.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-middle-east-12679902 |title=Syrian activist Haitham al-Maleh freed under amnesty |publisher=BBC News |date= |accessdate=2011-03-08}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.reuters.com/article/2011/03/08/us-syria-rights-idUSTRE7274LC20110308 |title=Syria frees 80-year-old former judge in amnesty |publisher=Reuters |date= |accessdate=2011-03-08}}</ref>

On March 9, Former Defense Secretary [[Donald Rumsfeld]] released a video given to him by [[Saddam Hussein]] nearly two decades ago. Young Syrians are shown biting the heads off snakes and stabbing puppies to applause from [[Hafez al-Assad]], Syria's president for three decades until his death in 2000. Rumsfeld called the Syrian regime "barbaric".<ref>{{cite web|url=http://abcnews.go.com/Politics/donald-rumsfeld-video-saddam-hussein-depicts-barbaric-syrian/story?id=13087737 |title=Saddam Hussein's Gift to Donald Rumsfeld: Video of Syrian 'Atrocities' |publisher=ABC |date= |accessdate=2011-03-09}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.seattlepi.com/politico/436740_politico50861.html |title=Rumsfeld releases Saddam video |publisher=seattlepi.com |date= |accessdate=2011-03-09}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IGut9LbvMlg |title=وحشية النظام السوري وحافظ في فيديو نادر جداً صدر اليوم |publisher=Youtube |date= |accessdate=2011-03-09}}</ref> At the same time, [[Bashar al-Assad]] seeked [[United Nations Human Rights Council]] seat, one week after [[Qaddafi]] was ousted. <ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.unwatch.org/site/apps/nlnet/content2.aspx?c=bdKKISNqEmG&b=1316871&ct=9134721 |title=Syria's Assad seeks UN rights council seat after Qaddafi ousted
 |publisher=unwatch.org |date= |accessdate=2011-03-09}}</ref>

On February 14, blogger and student [[Tal al-Mallohi]] was sentenced to five years in prison for spying for the United States. Washington denied these allegations and asked for the release Tal immediately. February 15, under pressure from human rights organizations, the Syrian government released ''Ghassan al-Najjar'', a "veteran Islamist activist" after he went on hunger strike following his arrest for calling for mass protests.<ref>{{cite news | url = http://www.reuters.com/article/2011/02/16/us-protests-idUSTRE71F41K20110216?pageNumber=2 | title = Egypt-inspired protests gain pace across region | first = Paul | last = Taylor | date = 2011-02-16 | publisher = Reuters }}</ref> February 16, regime critic and director of the Organisation for Democracy and Freedom in Syria (ODFS) [[Ribal al-Assad]], the son of [[Rifaat al-Assad]] and cousin to Syrian President [[Bashar al-Assad]] held a press conference in London, where he made it clear that he "does not want to see a Syrian revolution, but a peaceful change of power".<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.elaph.com/Web/news/2011/2/632409.html |title=Opposition leader Ribal Al-Assad is optimistic to changes in Syria |publisher=elaph.com |date= |accessdate=2011-02-16}}</ref>
